original_hypothesis.title: Stop policy accepts arbitrary RentalOrder data (no on-chain existence check) enabling asset theft via early reclaim + forced revert
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Stop.stopRent() and stopRentBatch() accept a full RentalOrder struct from an untrusted caller but do not verify that the order exists in Storage before performing external interactions. The only existence check is implicitly deferred to STORE.removeRentals(), which is called after hooks, safe delegatecall reclaim, and escrow settlement. An attacker can craft an order referencing a protocol-deployed Safe they own (or any Safe with Stop module enabled), include any NFTs currently held by that Safe as 'rental items', and force the Safe to delegatecall reclaimRentalOrder(), which will transfer those NFTs to the attacker-chosen 'lender'. The attacker can then intentionally make STORE.removeRentals() revert (e.g., by using a non-existent orderHash), but because the Safe execTransactionFromModule call is a separate internal transaction whose effects are not reverted by Stop's later revert if the Safe call succeeds, this could result in permanent NFT loss from the Safe (requires validation of Safe module call atomicity and whether Stop reverts after Safe call).
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete unprivileged on-chain path: stopRent is public and performs a delegatecall-based asset transfer from a Safe before confirming an active order. If the Safe module execution is not fully rolled back by the Stop revert (or if the Safe call returns success even when internal token transfers succeed), this becomes direct theft. Even if atomicity holds, the pattern is still an operationally critical DoS/griefing vector because it executes expensive external calls before an existence check.
deployment_assumptions: Rental Safes have Stop policy enabled as a Safe module (Factory.initializeRentalSafe).
Gnosis Safe module execution side effects may persist even if the caller (Stop) later reverts (must be verified against Safe implementation and call semantics).
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Factory.sol indicates Stop module is enabled on deployment via initializeRentalSafe(); no explicit pre-check exists in Stop.stopRent prior to reclaim/settlement.

proof_of_signal.lines: stopRent(): external calls to _removeHooks/_reclaimRentedItems/ESCRW.settlePayment occur before STORE.removeRentals
proof_of_signal.pattern: No `STORE.orders[hash]` check before delegatecall reclaim; existence check deferred to `STORE.removeRentals(_deriveRentalOrderHash(order), ...)`

preconditions: Target Safe can execute Stop module (module enabled) and holds NFTs the attacker wants to move.
Attacker can craft RentalOrder such that Safe delegatecall to reclaimRentalOrder transfers those NFTs to attacker-controlled lender address.
There exists a way for Stop.stopRent to revert after the Safe call while not reverting the Safe side effects (must be validated).

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Externalized state transition: reclaim and payments happen before verifying Storage.orders[orderHash] exists
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time a Safe has Stop module enabled and holds transferable NFTs

operational_questions: Does Safe.execTransactionFromModule() side effects revert if Stop reverts later in the same outer call?
Can an attacker construct an order that makes Safe call succeed but STORE.removeRentals revert reliably?
Are there other stateful external calls (hooks, ERC20 transfers) that can cause partial completion before revert?
validation_requirements: End-to-end test: call stopRent with fake order that triggers successful reclaim then revert on removeRentals
Review SafeL2 execTransactionFromModule atomicity and return semantics
Simulate with ERC721 that emits events and changes ownership to detect persistence after revert

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify a protocol-deployed rental safe (STORE.deployedSafes != 0) that has Stop module enabled and holds NFTs
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Craft RentalOrder with items listing those NFTs and set lender=attacker address; choose orderType such that _validateRentalCanBeStoped passes (e.g., BASE with endTimestamp <= now)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call Stop.stopRent(order) to trigger _reclaimRentedItems delegatecall transferring NFTs out
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Force STORE.removeRentals to revert by ensuring derived orderHash was never added (or rentalAssetUpdates mismatch)
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None on-chain beyond having a target Safe with Stop module enabled

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Potential theft of NFTs from Safes or severe griefing/DoS if atomicity prevents theft
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High if NFTs are transferred and state cannot be reconciled

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time after endTimestamp (or PAY order lender path if applicable)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed stopRent txs that nevertheless coincide with NFT Transfer events from rental safes
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Repeated stop attempts with invalid order hashes
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Stop called by unrelated addresses for many different safes

validation_result.mechanism: CEI violation with deferred existence check
validation_result.impact_summary: The described asset theft is impossible because EVM transaction atomicity guarantees that if STORE.removeRentals() reverts (on non-existent order hash), ALL state changes in the transaction—including NFT transfers from the Safe—are rolled back.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis claims that Stop.stopRent() performs external interactions (hooks, Safe delegatecall reclaim, escrow settlement) before verifying the order exists in Storage, and that an attacker could craft a fake order to transfer NFTs out of a Safe, then force STORE.removeRentals to revert while the Safe's side effects persist. This is incorrect due to fundamental EVM transaction atomicity. In Ethereum, when any part of a transaction reverts, ALL state changes within that transaction are rolled back—including those from external calls, delegate calls, and subcalls. There is no mechanism by which Safe.execTransactionFromModule's effects can persist if the outer Stop.stopRent call later reverts. The precondition 'There exists a way for Stop.stopRent to revert after the Safe call while not reverting the Safe side effects' is provably false by EVM design. Additionally, for most crafted fake orders, the transaction would revert even earlier at ESCRW.settlePayment due to balanceOf underflow when attempting _decreaseDeposit for tokens never deposited.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The CEI violation pattern IS real—Stop.sol does perform external calls before the existence check in STORE.removeRentals. However, this pattern cannot be exploited for asset theft because the entire transaction is atomic. The existence check at Storage.sol removeRentals() (line: `if (!orders[orderHash]) { revert ... }`) acts as a final gate that, upon revert, rolls back ALL preceding state changes in the same transaction.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: CEI violation is present: external calls (_removeHooks, _reclaimRentedItems, settlePayment) occur before the storage existence check in STORE.removeRentals. However, this does NOT enable asset theft because EVM transaction atomicity ensures all state changes revert if STORE.removeRentals reverts. The pattern is a code quality concern (wasted gas, reentrancy surface) but not exploitable for theft.

calling_sequence: 4. _reclaimRentedItems: ISafe(order.rentalWallet).execTransactionFromModule -> delegatecall reclaimRentalOrder -> safeTransferFrom NFTs to lender
6. STORE.removeRentals: checks orders[orderHash] == true, reverts if not
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: If a fake order is submitted: Steps 3-5 execute external calls. Step 5 would likely revert first due to balanceOf[token] underflow in _decreaseDeposit for tokens never deposited. Even if steps 3-5 all succeed, step 6 reverts because the order hash doesn't exist in storage. Upon revert at ANY step, EVM atomicity guarantees ALL state changes from ALL prior steps are rolled back. The Safe's NFT transfers from step 4 do NOT persist.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Rental Safes have Stop policy enabled as a Safe module (Factory.initializeRentalSafe).
Gnosis Safe module execution side effects may persist even if the caller (Stop) later reverts.
config_validation.deployment_evidence: Factory.sol initializeRentalSafe() confirms Stop policy is enabled as module via ISafe(address(this)).enableModule(_stopPolicy). DeployProtocol.s.sol shows _deployStopPolicy and _setUpPolicy(address(stop)) are called. However, the second assumption about Safe side effects persisting after caller revert is FALSE by EVM design.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack fails at the fundamental EVM level. Step-by-step: (1) Attacker crafts a fake RentalOrder with rentalWallet=target_safe, lender=attacker, valid orderType/endTimestamp. (2) Calls stopRent(fakeOrder). (3) _validateRentalCanBeStoped passes since it only checks orderType + timestamp + msg.sender vs lender. (4) _removeHooks: if order.hooks is empty, skipped. (5) _reclaimRentedItems: Safe.execTransactionFromModule succeeds since Stop is a module, delegatecall transfers NFTs. (6) ESCRW.settlePayment: attempts _decreaseDeposit on balanceOf[token] for fake ERC20 items—this underflows and REVERTS (Solidity 0.8.x checked arithmetic). Even if no ERC20 items exist in the fake order, step (7) STORE.removeRentals reverts with OrderDoesNotExist because the fake order hash was never stored. (8) The revert propagates up, and ALL state changes including NFT transfers are rolled back. Net result: attacker pays gas, nothing happens.
validation_result.impact_assessment: No impact. The described attack vector is impossible due to EVM transaction atomicity. The CEI violation is a code quality issue that creates unnecessary gas waste for invalid orders and theoretically widens the reentrancy surface, but no exploitable reentrancy path was identified.
recommended_mitigations: Move the STORE.orders[orderHash] existence check to the beginning of stopRent() before any external calls, following CEI pattern. This would save gas on invalid orders and eliminate the theoretical reentrancy surface.
Add an explicit order existence check: require(STORE.orders[_deriveRentalOrderHash(order)], 'order not found') as the first operation after building the hash.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy protocol with a rental Safe holding an NFT under active rental. 2. Craft a fake RentalOrder with the Safe's address as rentalWallet, attacker as lender, expired endTimestamp. 3. Call stopRent(fakeOrder). 4. Observe that the entire transaction reverts (either at settlePayment due to balanceOf underflow or at removeRentals due to non-existent order). 5. Verify NFT remains in the Safe after the reverted transaction.
uncertainties: Gnosis Safe's execTransactionFromModule implementation was not provided in scope, but its behavior as a standard external call within the EVM is well-established—it does not create a separate atomic context.
additional_testing_needed: Verify that crafted fake orders with no ERC20 payment items still revert at STORE.removeRentals (they should, since the hash won't exist).
Test reentrancy scenarios where the lender is a contract receiving ERC721 callback during legitimate stopRent, attempting to re-enter stopRent for the same or different orders.
